data.title: Genfit adds $20M as Ipsen records strong sales for liver drug
data.description:
Genfit (GNFTY) (GNFTF) closed higher in Europe on Thursday after the French biotech said that it is entitled to a $20M payment from partner Ipsen (IPSEY) due to the stronger-than-expected commercial performance of Iqirvo, their jointly developed liver drug.

In 2024, under the FDA’s accelerated pathway for approvals. Ipsen (IPSEF) received the U.S. nod for the oral, once-daily therapy as part of a combination regimen for primary biliary cholangitis ((PBC)), an autoimmune disorder affecting bile ducts.

Ipsen (IPSEY), which reported its Q4 results on Thursday, posted $88M and $208M in net sales for Iqirvo in PBC for the quarter and full year, respectively.

The better-than-expected sales performance has led to the first commercial milestone payment under a licensing deal signed by the companies in 2021, Genfit (GNFT) said, adding that the payment occurred ahead of its initial plans.

Additionally, the company said that Ipsen (IPSEY) confirmed the initiation of a Phase 3 trial for the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor ((PPAR)) agonist against primary sclerosing cholangitis, a rare liver disease with no approved therapies.

“We are pleased with Ipsen’s continued success with Iqirvo,” Genfit (GNFT) CEO Pascal Prigent remarked, adding, “We believe that this commercial trajectory has the potential to be transformative for our business model.”

data.title: Granite Construction targets 6%–8% organic growth in 2026 as acquisitions and margin expansion drive outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Granite Construction Incorporated (GVA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Kyle Larkin stated that "In 2025, we continue to focus on bidding and building the right projects, investing in our materials business and expanding our geographic footprint through targeted M&A." Larkin highlighted a disciplined approach to project selection, emphasizing high-quality bid build opportunities in home markets. The company grew its committed and awarded projects (CAP) to a record $7 billion at year-end 2025, the highest in its history. He pointed to a strategy shift since 2020, which has increased Construction segment gross profit margin from 8.8% in 2020 to 15.7% in 2025, with organic top line growth. Larkin also noted ongoing investments in the Materials segment, citing a jump in cash gross profit from 19% in 2023 to 26% in 2025, and confirmed plans to spend another $50 million in strategic CapEx in the materials business in 2026. Three 2025 acquisitions—Warren Paving, Papich Construction, and Cinderlite—expanded the company’s Southeast platform and home markets in California and Nevada. Larkin described these as "margin accretive acquisitions and strong growing markets" and indicated acquisition activity would remain a major growth component in 2026.
  • Larkin said, "We believe we are on track to achieve our 2027 financial targets supported by favorable market conditions, robust infrastructure funding and consistent execution across the business."
  • CFO Staci Woolsey reported, "2025 was a tremendous year of growth with year-over-year increases in a number of areas. Revenue increased 10% to $4.4 billion. Gross profit increased 24% to $711 million, adjusted net income increased 29% to $276 million. Adjusted EBITDA increased 31% to $527 million and operating cash flow increased 3% to $469 million."

Outlook

  • The company expects 2026 revenue in the range of $4.9 billion to $5.1 billion, which includes a full year of 2025 acquisitions. Woolsey stated, "We expect revenue to grow to a range of $4.9 billion to $5.1 billion. This reflects our record CAP balance and the strong macro environment and places organic growth at the high end of our 2027 target CAGR of 6% to 8%."
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin for 2026 is projected in the range of 12% to 13% of revenue. Woolsey explained, "With our high-quality CAP portfolio, strong market and high-performing materials business, we expect continued adjusted EBITDA margin expansion, in line with our 2027 financial target of 12.5% to 14.5% of revenue."
  • SG&A is targeted at 8.5% to 9% of revenue, including $48 million in stock-based compensation expense. The company plans CapEx of $140 million to $160 million, with about $50 million allocated to the materials segment.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for 2025 reached $4.4 billion, up 10% year-over-year. Gross profit rose 24% to $711 million. Adjusted net income was $276 million and adjusted EBITDA $527 million, with operating cash flow of $469 million.
  • Construction segment revenue in Q4 increased $119 million or 14% year-over-year to $940 million. Newly acquired companies contributed $59 million in Construction segment revenue. Construction segment gross profit reached $143 million, with a segment gross profit margin of 15%.
  • Materials segment revenue increased $69 million year-over-year to $225 million; gross profit for the segment rose by $2 million to $25 million. Materials cash gross profit for the quarter climbed $10 million year-over-year to $47 million or 21% of revenue. Full-year cash gross profit margin improved 490 basis points year-over-year to 26%.
  • The company ended 2025 with $650 million in cash and marketable securities and $1.3 billion in debt.

Q&A

  • Brent Thielman, D.A. Davidson: Asked about federal infrastructure funding and the outlook post-IIJA expiration. Larkin responded, "There's still a really nice runway of spending to go. So that will last likely for a few more years. I think what we hear really from industry today is that there's still bipartisan support... the investment amount is significantly higher... than what is in the IIJA."
  • Thielman followed up on direct federal opportunities and border infrastructure. Larkin explained, "We actually have one contract today in Southeastern Texas... just under $200 million. And we started that work last November. So there is a huge program and opportunity in front of us." He clarified, "We don't have any additional border infrastructure work in the guidance that we provided you today."
  • Steven Ramsey, Thompson Research Group: Inquired about CAP targets and dependency on current levels. Larkin said, "The $7 billion is a really high-quality CAP. The margin profile within our CAP continues to improve, and that's going to also get us to those 2027 targets."
  • Ramsey asked about strategic CapEx allocation and Warren integration. Woolsey said, "That's more heavily weighted towards the legacy business and expanding reserves and doing automation projects there... Warren Paving is off to a strong start... all 3 of our acquisitions last year are performing very well."
  • Kevin Gainey, Thompson, Davis: Asked about project bidding opportunities by vertical. Larkin responded, "The market is strong. It's been strong. It remains strong... The public market with the IIJA is still a big part of our business, around 85% or more today."
  • Gainey followed up on operational concerns with high CAP. Larkin said, "We don't have any execution concerns at all in that regard."
  • Gainey asked about EBITDA guidance range. Larkin cited weather, execution, and new work wins as factors, stating, "We tend to outperform our projects more than we underperform today."
  • Michael Dudas, Vertical Research Partners: Asked about best value contract backlog and future visibility. Larkin said, "As more states pass legislation to allow CMGC or CMAR or progressive design build, we could see that increase. And I think that's okay. It allows us to do some more complex larger contracts in a derisked manner."
  • Dudas asked about materials pricing. Larkin projected "mid-single-digit price improvements on the ag side and low single digit on the asphalt" for 2026. On cost control, he added, "Costs year-over-year actually been flat... automation efforts we put in place, the standardization of our materials playbook."
  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs: Probed margin expansion drivers and M&A pipeline. Larkin broke down margin expansion between construction and materials, noting, "It's about a 70 basis point improvement between Construction and Materials... about a 50 basis point improvement on SG&A... nets out to about a 50 basis point improvement." On M&A, he noted, "I do expect and we expect to get several things done this year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability of growth, CAP quality, federal infrastructure funding, and integration/execution risk; the tone was neutral to slightly positive, with multiple questions on guidance clarity and margin drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing operational discipline, positive acquisition integration, and strong market conditions. Larkin stated, "I have strong confidence in the future of Granite."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, confidence remained high but Q4 featured more detailed discussion of execution risks and margin levers, with management addressing analyst questions directly and providing specific forward-looking commentary.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was updated to a revenue range of $4.9 billion to $5.1 billion for 2026, compared to the prior quarter’s annual target of $4.35 billion to $4.45 billion for 2025. Adjusted EBITDA margin guidance for 2026 was set at 12% to 13%, up from 11.5% to 12.5% for 2025.
  • Strategic focus shifted further to M&A-driven growth, with the Warren Paving, Papich Construction, and Cinderlite acquisitions emphasized as outperforming expectations. The CAP reached a record $7 billion, up from $6.3 billion at Q3’s end.
  • Analysts’ questions advanced from acquisition execution to in-depth margin drivers, federal project pipeline, and CAP conversion. Management continued to express confidence, with slightly more discussion of execution and risk management in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged weather as a potential risk to execution and margin realization, especially in Q1 and Q4. Larkin noted, "weather can always be an opportunity or it can be a hindrance for us."
  • The risk profile for federal border infrastructure contracts was noted as changing, prompting a more disciplined approach to project pursuit.
  • No operational concerns were expressed regarding executing on the $7 billion CAP, and management described cost control measures and integration efforts as robust.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored that Granite Construction is executing on its strategy of focused project selection, disciplined M&A, and investment in high-margin materials businesses. With a record CAP, strong cash generation, and a proven track record of integrating acquisitions, the company expects to achieve organic revenue growth at the upper end of its 6%–8% target in 2026, expand margins, and deliver on its 2027 financial goals, all while maintaining a confident outlook on both public and private market opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BMO Junior Gold Index ETF Leads the Pack with RSI of 100: Insights into Precious Metals ETFs
data.description:
Gold and Silver prices fell during intraday trading on Thursday, as the commodities market continued to battle volatility in 2026.

In light of this, below is a list of the top 10 precious metals ETFs in the gold and precious metals sector ranked based on their RSI or Relative Strength Index.

The list is topped by BMO Junior Gold Index ETF (BMJJF), with a RSI of 100. VanEck Gold Miners UCITS ETF USD A (GTXAF) and iShares S&P/TSX Global Gold Index ETF (IUTCF) are next, with Global X Gold Producer Equity Covered Call ETF (HEPZF) and Sprott Junior Gold Miners ETF (SGDJ) rounding out the rest of the top five.

Several gold miner funds maintain an RSI of 60, including VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX), Sprott Gold Miners ETF (SGDM), VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ), and iShares MSCI Global Gold Miners ETF (RING).

The Relative Strength Index is a momentum oscillator that measures the velocity and magnitude of price changes, plotting them on a scale from 0 to 100. RSI compares the magnitude of recent gains to recent losses over a chosen lookback period, typically 14 days.

Here is the list:

  1. BMO Junior Gold Index ETF (BMJJF), RSI: 100

  2. VanEck Gold Miners UCITS ETF USD A (GTXAF), RSI: 95

  3. iShares S&P/TSX Global Gold Index ETF (IUTCF), RSI: 93

  4. Global X Gold Producer Equity Covered Call ETF (HEPZF), RSI: 88

  5. Sprott Junior Gold Miners ETF (SGDJ), RSI: 61

  6. Global X Gold Miners ETF (AUAU), RSI: 60

  7. VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX), RSI: 60

  8. Sprott Gold Miners ETF (SGDM), RSI: 60

  9. VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ), RSI: 60

  10. iShares MSCI Global Gold Miners ETF (RING), RSI: 60

data.title: Trucking and logistics stocks are swept into the AI anxiety sell-off
data.description:
There was broad selling pressure in the transportation sector on Thursday on AI-related concerns that have exploded over the last few weeks over companies that could be at risk of industry disruption.

Baird's Daniel Moore was just one of the analysts who highlighted growing concerns that open-source automation agents such as Molt Bot could level the tech-related playing field for smaller operators.

Separately, traders are evaluating the impact of U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy’s ruling to prohibit  "unqualified foreign drivers" from obtaining licenses to drive commercial trucks and buses in a move that has met opposition in the trucking industry.

Trucking stocks on the move included RXO Inc (RXO) -26.5%, Universal Logistics Holdings Inc (ULH) -13.3%, TFI International Inc (TFII) -10.7%, PAMT Corp (PAMT) -9.9%, ArcBest Corp (ARCB) -9.5%, Werner Enterprises Inc (WERN) -9.3%, Heartland Express Inc (HTLD) -9.2%, Covenant Logistics Group Inc (CVLG) -8.7%, XPO Inc (XPO) -7.2%, Schneider National Inc (SNDR) -6.6%, Marten Transport Ltd (MRTN) -6.6%, Saia Inc (SAIA) -5.8%, Old Dominion Freight Line Inc (ODFL) -5.3%, and Knight-Swift Transportation Holdings Inc (KNX) -4.9%.

Meanwhile, integrated freight & logistics stocks on the move included C.H. Robinson Worldwide (CHRW) -22.5%, Landstar System (LSTR) -18.8%, Expeditors International of Washington  (EXPD) -14.8%, Proficient Auto Logistics  (PAL) -13.2%, Radiant Logistics (RLGT) -11.8%, Hub Group (HUBG) -10.9%, Forward Air Corp (FWRD) -9.2%, J.B. Hunt Transport Services (JBHT) -7.6%, CryoPort (CYRX) -5.2%, Freightos (CRGO) -4.4%, and Smart Logistics Global Ltd (SLGB) -3.9%.

data.title: Hermes International reports FY results
data.description:
• Hermes International press release (HESAY): FY consolidated revenue amounted to €16 billion in 2025, up 9% at constant exchange rates and 5.5% at current exchange rates compared to 2024. 
  • Net profit (group share) reached €4.5 billion, up 5.5%, excluding the exceptional contribution on the profits of large companies in France, atthe same pace as sales.

data.title: The market's ironic bull case may be an AI spending timeout: Marko Kolanovic
data.description:
In a twist for the AI trade, markets (SP500) (SPY) (QQQ) could bounce if a major tech player reins in AI infrastructure spending and pivots toward protecting margins and cash generation, Marko Kolanovic, former chief strategist and co-head of Global Research at J. P. Morgan, said on Thursday.

Software (IGV) (XSW) (IGV) has been hit hard this year in a broader tech-led selloff that has been pricing in a mix of AI disruption fears and a separate worry that hyperscalers' spending plans keep rising to potentially unsustainable levels. Meanwhile, a pronounced rotation into value stocks has emerged as one of the defining market themes of 2026

"To rally, ironically, market will need a hyperscaler or a software company to say they will stop investing in AI (stop buying inflated memory) and refocus on cash flow," Kolanovic said in a post on social media platform X.
 
Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN) and Meta Platforms (META) have released plans to spend a combined $650B on AI this year.

data.title: Bruker outlines 15–17% EPS growth and targets 250–300 bps margin expansion for 2026 amid cost initiatives and market recovery
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bruker Corporation (BRKR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Frank Laukien, Chairman, CEO & President, reported that despite a challenging 2025 with headwinds from academic funding, tariffs, and currencies, "we delivered revenues ahead of our expectations" in Q4. The BSI segment book-to-bill was again over 1.0x, suggesting the company is past the trough in demand seen mid-2025. Laukien highlighted, "we saw a strong free cash flow in Q4 over $200 million after admittedly weaker cash flow earlier in 2025."
  • Laukien explained that ELITech and Chemspeed acquisitions delivered mid- to high single-digit organic revenue growth, while NanoString was flat due to U.S. academic funding pressure. However, "spatial biology, including NanoString, orders were up in the double-digit percentages organically in the fourth quarter of 25% year-over-year."
  • On innovation, Laukien stated, "our innovation engine continued to shine in 2025 with outstanding and very competitive product launches... Many of these recent launches have seen strong initial demand, which we expect to drive revenue growth in fiscal year '26 and beyond."
  • Laukien outlined the 2026 guidance: "reported revenue growth of 4–5%, with 1% to 2% organic revenue growth for the full year and an approximate 1.5% revenue growth contribution from M&A." He added, "we aim for 250 to 300 bps operating profit margin improvement in '26 despite and including a 50 bps currency headwind."
  • Gerald Herman, Executive VP & CFO, provided context: "revenue for the quarter came in about $20 million above our guide expectations. However, despite the top line outperformance our non-GAAP operating margin of 15.7% came in below our expectations by about 100 basis points." Herman highlighted, "we delivered over $100 million in improved working capital performance in the fourth quarter of '25 and with CapEx investments at $22.6 million drove free cash flow of $207.3 million in the fourth quarter of '25 up about $54 million over the fourth quarter of '24."

Outlook

  • The company is guiding for fiscal year 2026 reported revenue of $3.57 billion to $3.60 billion, representing reported growth of 4% to 5%, with organic revenue growth of 1% to 2%, plus acquisitions contributing 1.5%, and a 1.5% estimated currency tailwind.
  • Laukien indicated, "we expect a mid-single-digit organic revenue decline in Q1 of '26, primarily due to the strong Q1 '25 year-over-year comparison. After our first quarter this year, we now expect to resume organic revenue growth in the second quarter and for the remainder of the year."
  • Non-GAAP EPS for fiscal year '26 is guided in the range of $2.10 to $2.15, equating to non-GAAP EPS growth of 15% to 17%. The company expects a currency headwind of approximately 8% to fiscal year '26 EPS, implying constant exchange rate non-GAAP EPS growth of 23% to 25% compared to '25.
  • Non-GAAP operating margin expansion of 250 to 300 basis points is expected, primarily driven by cost-saving initiatives.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 reported revenues were $977.2 million, approximately flat year-over-year, including a currency tailwind of 4.1%, M&A growth of 0.8%, and an organic decline of 5.1%.
  • Non-GAAP operating margin was 15.7%, down 240 bps year-over-year. Non-GAAP diluted EPS was $0.59, down from $0.76 in Q4 of '24.
  • For the full year 2025, reported revenues increased by 2.1% to $3.44 billion, with an organic revenue decline of 3.7%. Non-GAAP operating margin was 12.6%, down 280 bps year-over-year.
  • Free cash flow in Q4 was $207.3 million, a record for the company.

Q&A

  • Puneet Souda, Leerink Partners: Asked about margin drivers and cost initiatives. Laukien responded that cost reductions are now expected to yield closer to $140 million or higher annually, and "certainly by Q3, that should be all effective and then become annualized."
  • Michael Ryskin, BofA Securities: Inquired about confidence in margin expansion. Laukien stated, "we have a lot of confidence in that," attributing it to cost actions and supply chain improvements.
  • Tycho Peterson, Jefferies: Asked about end-market assumptions. Gerald Herman said, "for biopharma, we're not assuming a significant snapback. We're assuming low single digits organic growth. Our semi business... we're expecting to be in the low single digits in growth."
  • Subhalaxmi Nambi, Guggenheim: Asked about book-to-bill and backlog expectations. Laukien replied, "we believe actually that the book-to-bill trends over the last 2 quarters... will continue into this year also aided, of course, by easier comps."
  • Douglas Schenkel, Wolfe Research: Questioned Q1 organic revenue growth guidance, noting potential upside from delayed revenues and pricing. Herman clarified, "not all of that is going to impact in Q1. So I think we are expecting to see some improvement in Q2 from those, but not all of it hits in Q1 of '26."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a slightly negative to neutral tone, probing for clarity on margin drivers, order recovery, and end-market assumptions, with skepticism around Q1 guidance and cost savings timing.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, but was somewhat defensive when addressing Q1 margin and growth queries, using phrases like "we have a lot of confidence in that" and "we now expect these to yield on an annualized basis between closer to $140 million or even higher than that."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone in prepared remarks remains confident, while Q&A interactions reflect more caution and detailed explanations. Analyst tone has shifted from cautious optimism to a more pressing, detail-oriented skepticism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is now more specific, with explicit targets for revenue growth, EPS, and margin expansion. The previous quarter emphasized the need to wait for Q4 results before providing such detail.
  • Strategic focus has shifted from recovery and stabilization to active cost reduction and margin expansion, with cost initiatives increased above the previous $120 million target.
  • Analysts’ focus in the current quarter centered on margin expansion credibility, order momentum, and the timing of revenue recovery, while in the previous quarter, questions centered more on order trends and the sustainability of improved bookings.
  • Key metrics now highlight a return to organic growth and robust cost control, compared to prior concerns about persistent headwinds and weak demand.
  • Management’s confidence in cost actions and margin targets is more pronounced, though their responses to analysts indicate greater scrutiny and the need for detailed justification.

Risks and Concerns

  • Challenges mentioned include lingering uncertainty in the U.S. academic/government markets, foreign exchange headwinds, and the timing of order conversion to revenue.
  • Management's mitigation strategies focus on cost savings exceeding $140 million annually, portfolio innovation, and maintaining high backlog levels.
  • Analysts remain concerned about the credibility of margin expansion, pace of order recovery, and the sustainability of recent improvements in bookings, especially in the U.S. and China.

Final Takeaway

Bruker’s leadership has set clear and ambitious targets for 2026, including 4–5% reported revenue growth, 250–300 basis points of operating margin expansion, and 15–17% non-GAAP EPS growth, supported by cost initiatives expected to surpass $140 million annually. The company enters the year with a robust backlog, strong Q4 free cash flow, and positive momentum in order trends, particularly in spatial biology, diagnostics, and semiconductor metrology. While headwinds remain in academic/government markets and foreign exchange, management expresses confidence in overcoming these challenges, emphasizing operational discipline, innovation, and a return to organic growth in the second quarter and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Coincheck Group N.V. reports Q3 results
data.description:
• Coincheck Group N.V. press release (CNCK): Q3 Revenue of $915M.
  • Gross margin decreased 1%, to ¥3,833 million ($24.4 million) in the third quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to ¥3,886 million ($24.8 million) in the second quarter of fiscal 2026, partially offset by an increase in IEO revenue.
  • Verified Accounts increased 2%, to 2,475,345 as of December 31, 2025 from 2,421,080 as of September 30, 2025.
  • Customer Assets decreased 20%, to ¥948.5 billion ($6,049 million) as of December 31, 2025 from ¥1,189.2 billion ($7,584 million) as of September 30, 2025. Even though the quantity of digital tokens held by customers remained relatively stable during the third quarter of fiscal 2026, Customer Assets decreased due primarily to the decline in the market price of crypto assets, including Bitcoin, Ethereum and XRP.
  • Marketplace Trading Volume decreased 7%, to ¥87.7 billion ($559 million) for the third quarter of fiscal 2026 from ¥94.7 billion ($604 million) for the second quarter of fiscal 2026.
  • Net income increased 14% to ¥405 million ($2.6 million) in the third quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to ¥354 million ($2.3 million) in the second quarter of fiscal 2026. The primary driver of this increase was a decrease in income tax expense and an increase in gain from change in fair value of warrant liability in the second quarter of fiscal 2026.
  •

data.title: Kraft Heinz facing growth challenges as a single entity—J.P. Morgan
data.description:
Kraft Heinz’s (KHC) announcement to remain as a single entity eclipsed fourth quarter results Wednesday. But for J.P. Morgan’s Thomas Palmer and Brandon Cohen, the lackluster outlook and risks associated with the cost to drive growth warranted a downgrade to Underweight.

“We see ongoing challenges that could limit KHC’s expected volume inflection as 2026 progresses,” Palmer and Cohen said, adding that if the cost of driving growth for its underperforming segment proves higher than KHC anticipates (which has been the case for General Mills and others in the space), the company might find itself with less flexibility, given that the dividend should absorb nearly 80% of 2026 free cash flow.

The analysts cite North American volume decline of more than 3% in each of the past 19 quarters, despite investments in innovation, marketing, price, and brand renovation.

“The declines have been the result of both weaker category performance (which is less of a headwind lately than in the past) and market share losses (which show little signs of abating),” Palmer and Cohen write.

According to Nielsen data, volumes are trending lower in 13 of KHC’s largest U.S. retail categories, and the company is losing volume share in 10 categories, including packaged lunch meat.

Investments in marketing might be the “right choice” in the longer term, but these investments take time to translate into higher sales. KHC assumes these investments will start to ramp in the second quarter of this year and further into the second half and that volumes will closely follow.

But Palmer and Cohen are skeptical.

“On this basis, we wonder to what extent KHC’s 2026 outlook is conservative,” they say.

data.title: Antero Midstream outlines 8% EBITDA growth and $360M free cash flow target for 2026 following $1.1B HG Midstream acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Antero Midstream Corporation (AM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Michael Kennedy, President, CEO & Director, announced the closing of the HG Midstream acquisition for $1.1 billion, stating this bolt-on asset in the Marcellus shale "adds over 400 highly economic undeveloped locations dedicated to Antero Midstream that immediately compete for development capital and infrastructure projects in 2026." Kennedy emphasized the strategic fit of the acquisition and reaffirmed the company’s "just-in-time capital investment strategy that generates consistent and repeatable free cash flow."
  • Kennedy reported that EBITDA grew 7% year-over-year in 2025, marking "our 11th consecutive year of growth since our IPO in 2014." Free cash flow after dividends increased by 30%, driven by capital-efficient organic growth and throughput from Antero Resources. He projected continued EBITDA and free cash flow growth in 2026, with expectations for "8% year-over-year EBITDA growth and 11% year-over-year free cash flow growth."
  • Kennedy forecasted "another year of high single-digit EBITDA growth" in 2027 as benefits from the recent acquisition and synergies are realized, particularly from the integration of the water system and AR’s 3-rig new completion crew development program.
  • Justin Agnew, CFO, stated, "Adjusted EBITDA was $285 million during the quarter, which was a 4% increase year-over-year, driven by an increase in gathering and compression volumes." Agnew added, "We generated $85 million of free cash flow after dividends, which we used to reduce leverage to 2.7x and repurchased approximately $48 million of AM shares."
  • Agnew highlighted a company record for the year, noting "free cash flow after dividends of $325 million, which is a 30% increase compared to 2024," and a "20% return on invested capital or ROIC in 2025."
  • Agnew outlined the 2026 capital budget of $190 million to $220 million, allocating funds to well connect and water capital, construction and relocation of compression assets, integration of water systems, and expansion capital on dry gas acreage.

Outlook

  • For 2026, management is forecasting "adjusted EBITDA of over $1.2 billion at the midpoint or an 8% increase year-over-year."
  • Free cash flow after dividends is projected at $360 million, or "an 11% increase compared to 2025."
  • The company expects "further growth in the water business in 2027, as we begin servicing locations on HG acquired acreage."
  • Management reiterated a balanced return of capital program in 2026, stating, "we expect a balanced return of capital program in 2026 in the form of debt reduction and share repurchases. This allows us to maintain a strong balance sheet with leverage in the low 3x range."
  • No comparison to analysts’ estimates provided due to lack of sufficient data.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $285 million, a 4% year-over-year increase.
  • Free cash flow after dividends for the quarter was $85 million, which contributed to reducing leverage to 2.7x and repurchasing about $48 million in shares.
  • For the full year, free cash flow after dividends reached $325 million, a 30% increase from 2024, and the company reported a 20% ROIC.
  • The 2026 capital budget is set between $190 million and $220 million, with planned investments in water and dry gas infrastructure to "unlock significant optionality and improve reliability in the dry gas regime."
  • The company maintained an attractive $0.90 per share dividend.

Q&A

  • John Mackay, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.: "I want to start on the growth outlook. I understand '26 and '27 have some tailwinds from M&A in the headline numbers. Could you just walk us through a little bit what the kind of longer-term growth trajectory looks like, let's say, once the assets are kind of fully up and running, if you guys are running a 3-rig and 2 crew program?" Michael Kennedy: "That 3-rig, 2-rig program does provide continued growth even past '27. It's about a couple of hundred million a day of growth on throughput volumes... I think it'd still be in the mid- to high single-digit EBITDA growth like we've experienced over our time, our last 11 years and will have in '25 and '26. I think that's pretty fair to have us generate those type of growth in '27 and beyond."
  • John Mackay, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.: "On the AR side, you guys were talking about some growth upside plans... walk us through the thought process there. And then what that means for AM both from an EBITDA growth standpoint, but also a capital standpoint if you move to that higher potential target?" Michael Kennedy: "There's really no capital for AM outside of what we just outlined, Justin did. It's right in the heart of our field. We already have all the big trunk lines. We have whatever pipelines are necessary. We have the water... So really nothing different than these capital budgets that we've experienced over the past couple of years for AM."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability and trajectory of growth post-acquisition, with a neutral tone and specific questions regarding capital intensity and growth outlook beyond 2027.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently highlighting the company's track record and using phrases such as "we expect" and "positions us well."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management retained a similarly constructive and neutral-to-positive tone, with continued focus on growth and capital discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter features the closing of the $1.1 billion HG Midstream acquisition, a major development not present in the previous quarter.
  • Guidance for 2026 is more specific, with explicit projections for EBITDA and free cash flow growth, compared to the previous quarter's general statements about expanding free cash flow.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from in-basin demand and asset utilization in Q3 to long-term growth trajectory and capital allocation post-acquisition in Q4.
  • Management’s tone is consistently confident, with an increased emphasis on the benefits and synergies of the acquisition in this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the need to successfully integrate acquired assets and achieve expected synergies, particularly in water and dry gas infrastructure.
  • The company highlighted its reliance on "very modest capital budgets" to deliver growth, suggesting a risk if operational or market conditions require higher capital outlays.
  • No new or acute risks were raised by analysts during the Q&A session.

Final Takeaway

Antero Midstream delivered another year of growth, punctuated by the $1.1 billion HG Midstream acquisition, which immediately expands its asset base and sets the stage for continued EBITDA and free cash flow growth in 2026 and beyond. Management projects strong performance with an 8% EBITDA increase and 11% higher free cash flow after dividends for 2026, while maintaining disciplined capital allocation and a balanced return of capital program. The company’s strategy of integrating newly acquired assets and capitalizing on its existing infrastructure underpins its outlook for sustained, high single-digit growth into 2027, reinforcing confidence in its long-term value creation plan.
